content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="diuturni_silenti_1">: it was now more than six years since the defence of Milo, which was followed almost immediately by Cicero's absence as proconsul in Cilicia, whence he returned only on the eve of the Pharsalian campaign. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eram_usus_1"> , <gloss>had kept</gloss> <milestone n="214.3" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="verecundia_1"> , <gloss>modesty</gloss>, i.e. distrust of himself under the circumstances. <milestone n="214.4" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vellem_1">: not subj. of indir. question, but informal indir. disc.; § <ref n="AG 592">592</ref>, 2 (341, c); B. 323; G. 662; 11.649, ii (528, I); H.-B. 535, I, a. <milestone n="214.5" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tantam_mansuetudinem_1">, etc.: no doubt these words express the genuine and grateful surprise felt at Caesar's clemency, so different from the conduct of former conquerors in civil wars (as Sulla, for example). <milestone n="214.6" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rerum_omnium_1">, in <gloss>every respect</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_causa_2">: Cicero also had been on Pompey's side. <milestone n="214.15" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="versari_me_2">: this phrase belongs as well with <foreign xml:lang="lat">persuadere</foreign> as with <foreign xml:lang="lat">fas esse</foreign>, but its construction is determined by the latter. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_ipso_3"> , <gloss>in the case of many, and especially in my own</gloss>. <milestone n="214.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="paulo_ante_3"> , <gloss>just now</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="omnibus_3">, i.e. by pardoning Marcellus, whom he thought a most violent opponent, Caesar had shown that his clemency would extend to all, however much they had opposed him. <milestone n="214.26" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="suspicionibus_3">: Caesar is said to have suspected Marcellus of plotting his assassination (cf. sect. <ref n="Cic. Marc. 21">21</ref>). <milestone n="214.26" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ille_3">, i.e. Marcellus too. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="usurpare_5"> , <gloss>dwell on</gloss>. <milestone n="215.28" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="et_certe_5">, etc., <gloss>and it is certainly true that</gloss> etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Fortuna_6">: see Manil. Law, sect. <ref n="Cic. Man. 47">47</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hujus_gloriae_7">, i.e. the glory of granting this pardon. <milestone n="216.3" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="centurio_7">, the infantry officer (see note on <ref n="pl83.31">p. 83, l. 31</ref>). <milestone n="216.4" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praefectus_7">, the commander of the auxiliary cavalry. So <foreign xml:lang="lat"> cohors</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="lat">turma</foreign> correspond to each other, as the infantry and cavalry divisions. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="immanitate_barbaras_8">, i.e. <gloss>barbarous and fierce</gloss>: his first conquests had subdued the Gauls, Germans, and Britons. <milestone n="216.10" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="locis_infinitas_8">: Caesar moved from Gaul, <date when="-0049">B.C. 49</date>, into Italy, and the same year to Spain. In 48 he crossed over to Greece, and thence to Egypt; in 47 he carried on war in Asia Minor, and in 46 gained the crowning victory of Thapsus in Africa. <milestone n="216.13" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="animum_vincere_8"> , <gloss>to rule his spirit</gloss>; cf. Proverbs xvi, 32: "He that is slow to anger is better than the mighty; and he that ruleth his spirit than he that taketh a city." <milestone n="216.14" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="victoriam_temperare_8"> , <gloss>to control the passions of victory</gloss> (cf. sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 9">9</ref>, <ref n="pl216.27">p. 216, l. 27</ref>). <pb n="469"/> <milestone n="216.15" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nobilitate_praestantem_8">: this description is inserted to enhance the credit of Caesar's act, inasmuch as the greater the adversary the more dangerous his restoration would seem. <milestone n="216.17" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="haec_facit_8">: a slight break in the construction (anacoluthon); the proper predicate of the preceding infins. would be connected with them by <foreign xml:lang="lat">est</foreign>; the proper object of <foreign xml:lang="lat">facit</foreign> would be a result clause with <foreign xml:lang="lat">ut</foreign>. The effect of the Latin can be exactly reproduced in translation. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illae_quidem_9">: the pronoun (as often in concessive sentences) is inserted only to carry <foreign xml:lang="lat">quidem</foreign>, adding nothing to the sense; § <ref n="AG 298">298</ref>, a (195, c) ; H.-B. 274, 3. <milestone n="216.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nescio_modo_9">: here (as often) with a slight tone of regret; Cf. our colloquial "somehow or other." <milestone n="216.23" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tubarum_9">, <gloss>of trumpets</gloss>: the <foreign xml:lang="lat">tuba</foreign> was a long, straight horn, used in infantry; the <foreign xml:lang="lat">lituus</foreign> a curved one, used in cavalry. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_velis_10">: for this rare constr., see § <ref n="AG 571">571</ref>,c (332,f); Cf. G. 553,4; cf. H. 571, 2 (501, i, 2); Cf. H.-B. 521,3 and N. <milestone n="217.2" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="studiis_prosequemur_10">, the figure is that of a distinguished Roman escorted by a throng with enthusiastic acclamations (<foreign xml:lang="lat">studiis</foreign>). <milestone n="217.4" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hujus_curiae_10">: the old Curia Hostilia, on the north side of the <foreign xml:lang="lat"> Comitium</foreign>, was destroyed by fire in the riots after the death of Clodius, <date when="-0052">B.C. 52</date> (see Milo, sect. <ref n="Cic. Mil. 33">33</ref>); but was rebuilt by Faustus Sulla, son of the dictator. <milestone n="217.6" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="C_Marcelli_10">: Cos. <date when="-0050">B.C. 50</date>, cousin of M. Marcellus. <milestone n="217.10" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tropaeis_monumentis_11">: the <foreign xml:lang="lat">tropaea</foreign> were memorials of victory, consisting of armor of the conquered, arranged in human form, and either erected by itself or attached to some monument, as a column or arch. As to monuments, Caesar did not live to carry out his plans fully; he built, however, a new enclosure for assemblies, the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Saepta Julia</foreign>, and laid out a <pb n="470"/> new forum for courts of justice, the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Forum Julium</foreign>, north of the old Forum. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adlatura_sit_11">, though in form introduced by <foreign xml:lang="lat">ut</foreign>, is not the real result of <foreign xml:lang="lat">tanta est</foreign>, which should be some verb in sect. <ref n="Cic. Marc. 12">12</ref>; this, however, by a change in the construction, is suppressed, and the sentence begins again with a future (<foreign xml:lang="lat">florescet</foreign> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="operibus_12">, dat.: § <ref n="AG 381">381</ref> (229); B. 188, 2, d; G. 345; H. 426, 2 (385, 2); H.-B. 371. <milestone n="217.23" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="victores_12">, i.e. Cinna, Marius, and Sulla. <milestone n="217.25" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perinde_atque_12"> , <gloss>just as</gloss>. <milestone n="217.28" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="omnes_12">, <gloss>all of us</gloss> (as is shown by <foreign xml:lang="lat">sumus</foreign>). <milestone n="217.29" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="occidissemus_12"> , <gloss>had fallen</gloss>: a rhetorical exaggeration for "had forfeited our lives." </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illa_13">, i.e. Pompey's. <milestone n="218.7" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sibi_13">: see note on Cat. 2, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 2.17">17</ref> (<ref n="pl120.17">p. 120, l. 17</ref>). <milestone n="218.8" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reddidit_13"> , <gloss>restored</gloss>, by inspiring them with confidence that no vengeance would follow, so that they have returned to their homes. <milestone n="218.9" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hostis_13">, acc. plur. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ilagitantium_14">: before the outbreak of the Civil War, Caesar sent C. Curio (son of C. Curio, Verr. 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Ver. 1.1.18">18</ref>) to Rome with offers of compromise, which were spurned by the Senate. <milestone n="218.18" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hominem_14"> (emphat.), <gloss>the man</gloss> (Pompey), not his measures. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consilio_14"> , <gloss>reasons</gloss>. <milestone n="218.19" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="grati_animi_14">: at the time of Cicero's recall, Pompey interested himself to go in person to several of the Italian towns to encourage the general feeling in his favor, and so atoned in part for the tardiness of his support and his earlier hesitating, cold, and ungracious course. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="integra_re_15"> , <gloss>before anything had been done</gloss> (i.e. before peace was broken). <milestone n="218.24" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_periculo_15">: it is said that after Pompey's defeat the command was urged upon Cicero by Cato; and on his refusal to conduct the war, Sextus Pompey would have stabbed him unless Cato had interfered. <milestone n="218.27" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="statim_censuerit_15">: Cicero was welcomed and kindly treated by Caesar on his return to Italy, <date when="-0047">B.C. 47</date>. The war was not finished till the next year, hence <foreign xml:lang="lat">incertus exitus</foreign>, etc. <pb n="471"/> <milestone n="218.29" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="victor_15">, <gloss>when victorious</gloss> (opposed to <foreign xml:lang="lat">incertus</foreign>, etc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="certorum_hominum_16">: such senatorial leaders as Metellus, Scipio, and Dolabella. Cicero says, in a letter to M. Marius (Fam. 7.3): "Excepting the chief and a few besides, the others — the leaders I mean — were so grasping in the campaign and so cruel in their talk, that I shuddered at the thought of victory. There was nothing good except the cause." And to Atticus (Att. 9.7)," It is their plan to stifle (<foreign xml:lang="lat">suffocare</foreign>) the city and Italy by famine, then ravage the fields, set fire, and not spare the money of the rich." Pompey, he says, would often say, <foreign xml:lang="lat">Sulla potuit: ego non potero?</foreign> (Att. 9. 10). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_debeat_17">, clause of result. <milestone n="219.12" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="excitaret_17">: more exactly <foreign xml:lang="lat">excitaturus fuerit</foreign>; cf. § <ref n="AG 517">517</ref>, d (308, d, N.); B. 322; G. 597, R5, a; H. 541, N.1 (496, N.2); cf. H-B. 581, b, 2. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="otiosis_18">, <gloss>the neutral.</gloss> <milestone n="219.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="contulisse_ad_18"> , <gloss>made all hope</gloss>, etc., <gloss>depend on</gloss>, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="est_19">, <gloss> comes</gloss>. <milestone n="219.31" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_19">, <gloss>things which</gloss>. The Stoics held that virtue was the <foreign xml:lang="lat">summum bonum</foreign>, and Cicero here alludes to that doctrine. <milestone n="220.3" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="commodata_19"> , <gloss>loaned</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praesertim_20"> belongs with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="lapsis_20">. <milestone n="220.5" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="opinione_20"> , <gloss>notion</gloss>. <milestone n="220.6" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="specie_20">, etc., i.e. with the idea of following the apparently established government (that of the Senate, which was on Pompey's side). <milestone n="220.7" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_timuerunt_20">: cf. § <ref n="AG 572">572</ref>, b, N. (333, R.); the <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign> is logically the subject of <foreign xml:lang="lat">est</foreign>. <milestone n="220.8" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="senserunt_20"> , <gloss>found by experience</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="querelam_21">, etc., that the partisans of Pompey wished to kill him. <milestone n="220.16" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="de_tuis_21">, i.e. his immediate companions; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_uni_21">, those on the same side. <milestone n="220.17" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_fuerunt_21">, Sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">inimici</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nihil_cogitans_22"> , <gloss>inconsiderate</gloss> <milestone n="220.30" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="equidem_22">, <gloss>for my part</gloss> <milestone n="220.31" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dumtaxat_22"> , <gloss>merely</gloss> (i.e. even the ordinary chances of life, to say nothing of violence and plots). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="constituenda_judicia_23">, etc.: the short period of Caesar's dictatorship was distinguished by a number of salutary enactments, almost equivalent to a complete revision of the constitution. <milestone n="221.9" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="propaganda_suboles_23">: the waste of population by incessant wars had already begun to alarm the best minds of Rome. It was, in fact, the chief direct cause of the ruin of the Empire. <milestone n="221.10" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="diffiuxerunt_23"> , <gloss>have run wild</gloss> (like vines). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fuisset_24">, subj. of integral part. <milestone n="221.15" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="faceret_24">, in the same constr. as <foreign xml:lang="lat">perderet</foreign> - <foreign xml:lang="lat">prohibuisset</foreign>, cont. to fact apod.; the prot. is implied in <foreign xml:lang="lat">togatus</foreign>. <milestone n="221.16" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sananda_24"> , <gloss>to be healed</gloss> (referring to the result); </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mederi_24"> , <gloss>to remedy</gloss> (referring to treatment). <milestone n="221.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="doctorum_hominum_24"> , <gloss>philosophers</gloss>. <milestone n="221.25" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tam_24">, referring (as often) to the <foreign xml:lang="lat">protasis</foreign> which follows: § <ref n="AG 512">512</ref>, b (304, b); G. 590, N.1; H.-B. 578,5. <milestone n="221.29" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hic_24"> , <gloss>ill these circumstances</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="immo_vero_26"> , <gloss>on the contrary</gloss>. <milestone n="222.5" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="futurus_fuit_26"> , <gloss>was to be</gloss>. <milestone n="222.8" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_quidem_26"> , <gloss>since in fact</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 515">515</ref>, a, N. (306, a, N.); G. 595, R1; H. 574, I (507, 3, N.2); Cf. H-B. 582, 9. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hic_actus_27">, as in a play; hence <foreign xml:lang="lat">elaborandum</foreign>, a word used of literary composition. <milestone n="222.15" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dicito_27">, fut. as referring to the time designated by </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tum_27">: § <ref n="AG 449">449</ref>, I (269,d, I); B. 281, I, a; G. 268, 2; H. 560, 4(487, 21); H-B. 496. <milestone n="222.16" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="diu_27">: § <ref n="AG 33">33</ref> (29, c); G. 20, iii; H-B. 58, 3. <milestone n="222.19" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="angustus_27"> , <gloss>narrow bounds</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inservias_28">: § <ref n="AG 565">565</ref> (331,f R.); cf. B. 295, 8; cf. G. 553, a.1; cf. H. 554, ii,1 (502, I); cf. H.-B. 513, 5. <milestone n="222.25" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_quidem_28">, i.e. <foreign xml:lang="lat">aeternitas</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_miretur_28">, purpose clause; the subject is vita. <milestone n="222.27" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="certe_28"> , <gloss> doubtless</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="imperia_28">, etc., obj. of <foreign xml:lang="lat">audientes</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="lat">legentes</foreign>. 222 29 <foreign xml:lang="lat">munera</foreign>, <gloss>gifts</gloss> to the people, such as monuments and spectacular performances. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sedem_29"> , <gloss> abiding-place</gloss>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="domicilium_29"> , <gloss>home</gloss>. <milestone n="223.6" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="requirent_29"> , <gloss>will miss</gloss>. <milestone n="223.7" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fllud_29">, i.e. the war; hoc, i.e. the public safety. <milestone n="223.8" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="servi_judicibus_29"> , <gloss>pay regard to those judges</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_pertinebit_30"> , <gloss>will have no concern for</gloss>. Such was the doctrine of the Epicureans, who believed in annihilation after death. <milestone n="223.17" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="obscuritas_30"> , <gloss>uncertainty</gloss>, i.e. as to which side a good citizen ought to take. <milestone n="223.20" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="deceret_30"> , <gloss>was becoming</gloss>, i.e. to their position and circumstances. A Senator, for example, however well affected to Caesar, might have felt it his duty to side with his class (who were in general partisans of Pompey). Such conflicts of mind of course constantly arise in civil wars. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="liceret_30"> , <gloss>was</gloss> <foreign xml:lang="lat">legat</foreign> Both Caesar and Pompey claimed to be acting under the laws. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perfuncta_est_31"> , <gloss>has done with</gloss>. <milestone n="223.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="inllammaret_31">, etc.: cf. sect. <ref n="Cic. Marc. 16">16</ref> and note (<ref n="pl219.4">p. 219, l. 4</ref>). <milestone n="223.24" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ab_31">, etc.: the first <foreign xml:lang="lat">ab</foreign> means <gloss>by</gloss>; the second, <gloss>from</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sanitatis_32"> , <gloss>a sound mind</gloss> (ordinary intelligence). <milestone n="224.4" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="haec_32"> (with a gesture), <gloss>this glorious city</gloss>. <milestone n="224.9" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oppositus_32"> , <gloss>interposition</gloss>, literally plur.: § <ref n="AG 100"> 100</ref>, c (75, c); B. 55,4, c; G. 204, N.1; H. 138, 2 (130, 2); H-B. 240,5, b. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="unde_33"> , <gloss>with which</gloss> (in Latin the beginning is regarded as the source <gloss>from which</gloss>). <milestone n="224.11" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="agimus_33"> , <gloss> express</gloss>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="habemus_33"> , <gloss>feel</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mea_34"> , <gloss>on my pa;]</gloss>. <milestone n="224.22" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_praestiterim_34"> , <gloss>while I have fulfilled it</gloss> <milestone n="224.25" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="me_conservato_34"> , <gloss>while I have been preserved</gloss>. <milestone n="224.27" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_arbitrabar_34"> , <gloss>which I thought no longer possible</gloss>.</gloss>
